text:
Discovery interview — Revolut
Participant: Digital nomad, female, 29, freelance UX designer
Interviewer: Product researcher
Setting: Remote video call, ~45 minutes
Date: October 2025

---

Interviewer: Hi! Thanks so much for joining today. Before we start, can you tell me a bit about yourself and how you generally manage your money?

Participant: Sure, hi! So I'm Sofia, I'm a freelance UX designer, and I've been location-independent for about three years now. Right now I'm in Lisbon, but last year I spent six months in Mexico City and three in Bali.
The money thing has been... a journey, honestly. I started with a regular UK bank — HSBC — and that was a nightmare. They kept flagging my card every time I tried to buy a coffee in a new country.
Like, I get it, security, but I literally told them I was traveling. Multiple times. Anyway, a friend in Berlin told me about Revolut maybe two and a half years ago and I haven't really looked back.
Interviewer: Got it. So when you say you haven't looked back — does that mean Revolut is your primary account now? Participant: Yeah.
Well, mostly. I still have HSBC because I get paid into it sometimes — some clients are old-school and only do bank transfers to UK accounts. But like 80% of my actual life happens in Revolut.
Spending, transfers, even some of my savings. Interviewer: That's helpful. Can you walk me through what happened the first time you tried to send money internationally with Revolut?
Participant: Oh god, okay. So this was, um, maybe a year and a half ago? I had to pay rent in euros to my landlady in Berlin while I was still earning in pounds.

And I remember being really nervous because with HSBC the exchange rate was always — they would say "no fees" but then the rate was just terrible, you know? You'd lose like 4% just on the spread. So I tried Revolut, and I think I sent like fifty pounds first as a test, because I just didn't trust it.
And it arrived in like — I want to say two minutes? It was almost suspicious. I actually messaged my landlady to make sure it was real money.
Interviewer: Ha, that's a great way to put it. What was suspicious about it? Participant: Just — banks have always been slow.
Like, that's the whole experience of banks. You send money and it disappears for three days and you cross your fingers. So when something is instant, your brain goes "wait, where's the catch?"
The catch turned out to be nothing, really. I mean, on weekends the rate is slightly worse, which I learned later. But for what I needed it was fine.
Interviewer: You mentioned the rate. How aware are you, day to day, of the exchange rate Revolut is giving you? Participant: Honestly?
Not as aware as I should be. I check it sometimes. Like before a big transfer I'll glance at the rate.
But for everyday spending — like buying groceries in a different currency — I just trust it. I assume it's basically the market rate. I don't actually know.
I should probably check that. Interviewer: What would help you check it more easily? Participant: I mean, the in-app rate is shown when you're doing a transfer, which is good.
But when I'm just paying with the card, I have no idea what rate I got until the transaction shows up. And by then it's too late anyway. I guess... a notification?
Like "hey you just paid 12 euros at this rate"? But also that would be annoying probably. I don't know.
Interviewer: That's interesting. Let's talk about trust. You said you keep most of your life in Revolut now.
Do you trust it completely? Participant: Yes. I mean — yes.
Interviewer: Mostly? Participant: Okay so, here's a contradiction I have with myself. I say I trust it.
But I also keep a kind of emergency buffer in HSBC. Like maybe 800 pounds that I don't touch. Just in case.

And I know that's irrational because if Revolut went down HSBC isn't going to magically be available either, like there's no logical reason. But there's something about — I don't know, fintech is fintech, you know? It's an app.
My HSBC account has a physical building somewhere with people in it. That feels different even though logically it shouldn't. Interviewer: That's super interesting.
Have you ever had a problem with Revolut that made you nervous? Participant: Oh yeah. So this was probably eight months ago.

I was in Mexico City and I tried to buy a flight to Oaxaca with my Revolut card and it just got declined. And I tried again, declined. And then I checked the app and it said something like "your card has been temporarily blocked for your protection."

And I was just standing there in this airport like — okay. So I tried to use the chat support thing in the app, and the chat just... it wasn't really a chat. It was like a bot that kept asking me to verify things.

And then it said an agent would be with me in — I don't remember — like 30 minutes? And I'm at the airport. So I ended up paying with my HSBC card, which obviously also got flagged because of course it did, but then I called HSBC and they unblocked it in like 4 minutes because I could actually talk to a human.
Interviewer: How did the Revolut situation eventually resolve? Participant: I think someone replied like 90 minutes later? And by then I'd already missed the flight I was trying to book.
They unfroze the card. They didn't really apologize, they just said "your card is now active." Which — fine.

But yeah, that whole experience was really frustrating because I literally couldn't reach a human when I needed one. And the thing is, I get why they do it, I'm sure they prevent fraud all the time, but as the person in the airport you just feel completely powerless. Interviewer: After that, did anything change about how you use Revolut?
Participant: Yeah, I top up my HSBC card before I travel anywhere now. Just so I have a backup. So in a way I trust it less?
But I still use it. It's still my main thing because the alternative is just worse. Have you tried doing international transfers with HSBC?
It's like filling out a tax form. Interviewer: Have you considered alternatives like Wise or N26? Participant: Yeah, I have Wise actually.

I use it for receiving money from US clients sometimes because the rates on USD-GBP are slightly better. But Wise feels more like a tool, you know? Like I open it when I need to transfer something and then I close it.
Revolut is — it's a place I live. I check my spending, I have my budgets, I see my analytics. Wise doesn't feel like home.
N26 I tried briefly when I was in Berlin but I just never really got into it. The app felt clunky. Interviewer: You mentioned analytics.
How do you use those? Participant: I look at the spending breakdowns, like how much I spent on groceries vs going out vs travel. It's mostly to feel guilty, honestly.
Like at the end of the month I'll see "you spent 340 pounds on restaurants" and I'll be like, oh god. But I never actually change my behavior based on it. I just feel bad for an afternoon.
Is that useful? I don't know. Probably not.
Interviewer: What about savings? You mentioned you keep some savings in Revolut. Participant: Yeah, I have a vault.
I think it's called a Pocket now? Or a Vault? They renamed something.

I have one for taxes because as a freelancer that's a thing you have to think about. And I have one for "next move" which is just money I'm setting aside for when I move to the next country. The interface for that is fine.

I set up a recurring transfer when I get paid. The interest rate is okay, not amazing, but I'm not really doing it for the interest, I'm doing it because if it's in a vault I won't accidentally spend it. Interviewer: Have you ever considered Revolut Premium or Metal?
Participant: I had Premium for a few months. The lounge access was nice when I was traveling a lot. But honestly I couldn't justify the cost given how much I was actually using it.

I downgraded back to free. I think if I traveled even more I'd reconsider, but right now I'm staying put for stretches of three or four months at a time so the lounge thing isn't worth it. Interviewer: One last question.
If you could change one thing about Revolut, what would it be? Participant: Oh that's a good one. Customer support, hands down.
The product itself I really like. The transfers, the cards, the analytics, all of that — fine, good even. But the moment something goes wrong, you feel like you're shouting into a void.

And for a thing that holds your money, that's a really specific kind of stress. If I could pay a small monthly fee to get a real human within 5 minutes, I'd probably do it. Like that's how much I value it.
Interviewer: Has that ever made you consider switching back to a traditional bank? Participant: No. I mean, like I said, traditional banks are worse in pretty much every other way.
So I'm stuck. But "stuck because the alternatives are worse" isn't the same as loving something, you know? Interviewer: That's a great note to end on.
Thank you so much, Sofia, this was really useful. Participant: Yeah, of course! Hope it helps.

DISCOVERY LENS — SYNTHETIC USER INTERVIEW
Project: Asana Onboarding
Interview ID: INT-ASANA-01
Participant: Sarah K., Content Marketing Manager, ~30-person B2B SaaS company
Interviewer: Priya S., UX Researcher
Date: 2026-03-17
Duration: 44 minutes
Context: Sarah's team signed up ~6 weeks ago; completed first project within 3 weeks

---

[00:01:05]

PRIYA: Thanks for joining, Sarah. Let's just start from the beginning — how did your team end up on Asana? SARAH: So our CMO basically said we need a project management tool, here are three options, pick one.

And we picked Asana. Which I say like it was a group decision but honestly it was mostly me because I'd heard of it and the other two options were Monday.com and something I'd never heard of. So I just went with Asana because at least I had some mental model of it.
PRIYA: Had you used it before? SARAH: A little. Like at a previous job it was sort of around but I wasn't a power user.

I mostly just checked off tasks that other people assigned me. Setting it up myself was a completely different thing. I remember thinking — okay so I've seen this tool before and I still don't know how to set it up.
Which felt embarrassing. PRIYA: Why embarrassing? SARAH: Because I'm supposed to be organized.

That's literally part of my job. So admitting that a project management tool is confusing felt like a character flaw. I think that's partly why I spent so much time trying to figure it out myself before asking anyone.

I watched a bunch of YouTube videos actually. Not Asana's official ones, like third-party ones. Which is kind of funny in hindsight — the official help was probably fine but I trusted random people on YouTube more because they showed actual workflows.

[00:04:50]

PRIYA: Walk me through setting up your first project. SARAH: Okay so our first real project was a product launch campaign. Which, again, in hindsight was maybe too big.

It involved like six workstreams — email, social, blog, PR, paid, and events — and I tried to set up sections for each one. And then within each section I had tasks and subtasks and... it got complicated fast. The issue was I kept second-guessing the structure.
Like, is a blog post one task or multiple tasks? Is it one task per post, or one task per stage — brief, draft, review, publish? I kept changing my mind.

And every time I restructured things, the people I'd already assigned tasks to would get notifications and be like "why did my task move?" And I'd have to explain. PRIYA: How did the team respond to those changes?

SARAH: Not great. There was one person, Camille, who does our social content, and she came to me like week two and said "I don't know what I'm supposed to be doing in here." And I felt terrible because I'd sent her like three task reassignments in two days.

So I stopped reorganizing and just let the structure be imperfect. Which meant the project had this weird hybrid thing where some workstreams were in sections and some were in their own sub-projects and it was not clean. [00:09:20]

PRIYA: Did you ever feel like you got traction?

SARAH: Yeah, actually. The thing that changed it for us was — okay so I'm embarrassed I didn't do this earlier, but I booked a 30-minute team sync specifically to walk through Asana together. Like, here's how I've structured this, here's where your tasks are, here's how to mark things done.
And after that meeting things got way better. People started using it. I think before that meeting everyone was kind of tiptoeing around the tool.

PRIYA: What do you think happened in that meeting that made the difference? SARAH: I think people stopped feeling like they might do something wrong. Like there was a fear of — if I click the wrong button will I mess something up?

Will I delete someone's task? And just showing them that it's pretty hard to break things, and that there's an undo function, and that I could fix anything they messed up — that helped. Also I showed them where their tasks were specifically.

Not the whole project, just "here's your stuff." Once they could see their own name attached to things it clicked. [00:14:33]

PRIYA: What features caused the most confusion early on?

SARAH: Comments versus task descriptions. I did not understand for a long time that the description and the comment thread were two different things and that one was more for context and one was for back-and-forth. I put updates in the description for the first two weeks and then realized no one was seeing them because they weren't getting notifications for description edits.
So I'd update a brief in the description and then wonder why no one was responding. PRIYA: Did you figure that out on your own? SARAH: My colleague figured it out.
She came from a company that used Asana heavily and she said — oh you should be leaving a comment and tagging people. And I felt dumb. But also I feel like there should be a clearer UI distinction.

Like the description and the comment box look really similar? Also — and this is a totally different thing — the approvals feature. We have an approval workflow for every piece of content, everything goes through our head of content before it goes live.

I didn't know Asana had an approvals feature until like a month in. I was doing approvals over email this whole time. And when I found it I was genuinely annoyed because it was exactly what I needed and it had been there the whole time.
PRIYA: How did you find it? SARAH: I think I Googled "Asana approval workflow" and a blog post came up. Not from Asana's site.

Which is — look, I'm not trying to be critical, but if there's a feature that directly maps to a very common marketing workflow, maybe surface that earlier? Like during setup when it detects you're in marketing or when you add a content project? [00:20:11]

PRIYA: Let's talk about the first project completing.
What did that feel like? SARAH: Honestly? Anticlimactic.

I'm not sure I "completed" it so much as it just... ended. The campaign went live, and then the tasks were just kind of sitting there marked done. I didn't really know what to do with the project after.
Do I archive it? Do I leave it? I didn't want to delete it because the comments had useful context.
So it just sat there and now it's in my sidebar and every time I look at it I feel vaguely guilty. PRIYA: [laughs] Has that affected how you think about starting new projects? SARAH: A little.

I think I'm more thoughtful now about what "done" looks like before I start. Like the project needs a clear end state, not just a campaign launch but like, all tasks complete, retrospective done, project archived. But I wasn't thinking about that the first time.

[00:24:45]

PRIYA: What about integrations? Did you connect Asana to other tools? SARAH: We use Slack and Google Workspace, so I tried to connect those.

The Slack integration works okay — it will notify in a channel when a task is completed. We set that up for a channel called #campaign-updates and in theory the team can see progress without going into Asana. In practice I think three people muted that channel because it was too noisy.

So we turned off some of the notifications and now I'm not sure it does anything. Google Docs I tried to link in task descriptions, which is fine, but it's just a link, it doesn't tell you anything about the doc. Like if the doc has been updated I still have to go check it.
I kind of hoped it would be smarter than that. Like "this linked doc was edited yesterday" or something. Maybe that's too much to ask.
PRIYA: Any other tools? SARAH: We don't use any others for project management. But I will say — I looked at the integration library at some point and it's enormous.
Like there are hundreds of integrations. And I felt weirdly paralyzed. Like should I be connecting to all these things?

Am I missing something? I ended up doing nothing because choosing felt hard. [00:29:58]

PRIYA: Was there a moment where you almost switched to a different tool?

SARAH: Yeah. I was very close to saying let's just use Notion because at least I know Notion. The specific thing that almost made me switch was I couldn't figure out how to see across all my projects in one view.
Like I wanted a single place to see everything due this week across all my projects and I could not find it for a long time. I think it's in the "My Work" section? Or "Home"?

I keep forgetting what it's called. But at the time I couldn't find it and I thought — what is even the point of this if I still have to check each project individually. PRIYA: Did you find it eventually?
SARAH: Yes, but someone told me. A friend who uses Asana sent me a screenshot when I complained on Slack. And I was like — okay cool this is the thing I needed.

But it should not require a friend to tell me that exists. [00:34:12]

PRIYA: Has Asana changed how your team works? SARAH: I think so.

The thing I notice most is I get fewer status-update Slack messages. Before, people would DM me constantly like "hey is the blog draft done yet" and now they can just go look. That is genuinely better.

I do think the team trusts the tool more now than they did in the first few weeks. But also — and this is a contradiction maybe — I spend more time managing Asana than I expected. Like keeping the project clean, updating tasks, making sure descriptions have enough context.
That maintenance work wasn't something I budgeted for. It probably takes me 20-30 minutes a day? And I'm not sure that's offset by the time I save elsewhere yet.
Maybe eventually. PRIYA: Do you think that will change over time? SARAH: I think so.

I think part of it is that we're still building habits. Like we don't have a consistent process for how we write task descriptions or when to use subtasks versus tasks and those decisions still take mental energy every time. If we had clearer norms that would help.

[00:39:40]

PRIYA: Last question — what's one thing Asana could have done differently to make those first 30 days better? SARAH: I think I needed a real guided setup experience that was specific to my team's work, not generic. Like not "create your first task!"

but "you're setting up a marketing team, here's a project template for a campaign launch, here's how approvals work, here's how to connect your Slack channel." Just... make it relevant to me. The generic stuff felt like training wheels when I needed a bicycle.

Also — and I thought of this because you asked — when I added my team members, nothing happened. Like, they got an invite, they joined, and then... nothing told me what to do next. There was no "great, now orient your team" prompt.

I think a lot of my early pain would have been avoided if the product had said "now that your team is here, here's a 3-step checklist to make sure everyone knows what they're doing." PRIYA: That's really helpful. Thank you, Sarah.
SARAH: Of course. I hope it helps. And I want to say again — I do like Asana.

I'm not saying it's bad. But the early period was rocky and I think it didn't have to be. ---

[END OF TRANSCRIPT]

RESEARCHER NOTES (Priya S.):
- "Too embarrassed to ask for help" dynamic — self-sufficiency norm in marketing roles may create a silent onboarding failure mode
- Structure indecision led to constant reorganization → notification noise → team confusion; signals need for opinionated starting structure
- Team alignment meeting (week 2, 30 min) was the real activation event, not the product onboarding
- Approvals feature discovered via Google/blog, not in-product — feature-to-workflow matching gap
- "Anticlimactic completion" — no closure ritual or archiving prompt; project completion UX absent
- Cross-project view ("My Work") discovered via social network, not product — findability issue
- Contradiction: values reduced status DM noise (Asana working) but estimates 20-30 min/day maintenance overhead (not yet clearly net positive)
- Strong JTBD signal: "I need to stop being the status update hub for my team"

INTERVIEW 01 — LIDL PLUS APP
Participant: Sarah K., 28, Marketing Manager, London (Hackney)
Interviewer: M.
Duration: 42 minutes
Date: April 14, 2026

---

M: Thanks for making time. So just to start broadly — when did you first start using the Lidl Plus app? Sarah: God, um, maybe two years ago?

Honestly I downloaded it because the cashier kept asking me at checkout, "do you have the app, do you have the app," and I was like fine, I'll get the app. So it was kind of forced on me at first. But I do use it now.
I'd say I'm a regular user. M: Can you walk me through the last time you used it? Sarah: Yeah, so, last Saturday.

I went in for like, basics — pasta, some chicken, wine, the usual — and I opened the app at the till to scan the QR code. And honestly the worst part is I always forget to open it before I'm at the till, so there's like this awkward moment where I'm fumbling and there's a queue behind me and I'm trying to find the scan thing. The button.
Whatever it's called. The barcode. M: And did you use any coupons that visit?
Sarah: Um. Actually wait — yes, there was a money-off thing for, I think it was like the deli stuff? Or the bakery?
I can't remember. But I didn't actually buy any of those things so it didn't matter. M: Do you usually browse coupons before going shopping?
Sarah: [laughs] No. I know I'm supposed to. I know that's like, the whole point.

But who has time to sit there scrolling through coupons before going to the supermarket. I'm just trying to get in and out. Sometimes if I remember on the bus on the way there I'll have a quick look but mostly no.
M: When you do look, what's that experience like? Sarah: It's fine I guess. It's a bit of a — like there's loads of them and a lot of them are for things I'd never buy.
Like, why are you offering me money off cat food, I don't have a cat. So I have to scroll past a bunch of stuff. And the ones I do want, sometimes you have to like, "activate" them?
Which is annoying. Why isn't it just automatic. M: You mentioned activating — can you say more about that?

Sarah: Yeah so some coupons you have to tap to activate before you go shop, and if you forget — which I always do — then it doesn't apply at the till. I had this happen with a thing for, I think it was Pringles or something, and the coupon said £1 off but at the till it didn't come off and I was like, what? And I checked later and it was because I hadn't tapped activate.
Which is rubbish frankly. Just give me the discount. M: Has anything like that ever made you not trust the app?
Sarah: I mean — yeah but I still use it. Because the points add up. I cashed in a £5 voucher last month from points and that was nice.

But also I got like, an email or a notification or something saying I had reached some milestone and there was a scratch card thing and the prize was like 50p off, and I was like, you woke me up at 8am for 50p off coffee. So the notifications are a bit much. M: Do you get a lot of notifications from the app?
Sarah: Loads. I turned them off actually. Or — wait.
I think I turned them off. Maybe I just turned off the sound. I don't know.
They still pop up sometimes. There's one on a Thursday I think, the weekly offers? Which is actually useful.
So I haven't turned that off. M: You mentioned weekly offers — do you find those? Sarah: I see them in store more than in the app honestly.
The big yellow signs. The app version is fine but the in-store experience is, like, you go down the aisle and the things are just there with the discount. I don't really plan around it.
M: What about searching for products? Sarah: In the app? Can you search for products in the app?
I didn't know that. M: I think you can. Sarah: Oh.
I just go and look in the store. I wouldn't think to search in an app for whether they have, like, oat milk. I'd just go.
M: How would you compare Lidl Plus to other supermarket apps you've used? Sarah: I had Tesco Clubcard for ages. The Tesco one is, I think, more useful?

Because the Clubcard prices are everywhere in the store and it's clear. With Lidl it feels like the discounts are kind of hidden behind the app and you have to remember to do stuff. Also Tesco lets you pay through the app I think?
I don't use that. I have Aldi too — the Aldi app is — does Aldi have an app? I'm not sure.
I think it's just for the leaflet. M: You mentioned earlier you'd say you're a "regular user." How often do you actually open it outside of being at the checkout?
Sarah: Honestly? Maybe — once a week? Twice if I'm bored.
I don't really browse it. It's a checkout app for me. Open at the till, scan, close.
M: Earlier you said the coupons are a key part of why you use it. Sarah: Did I? I mean, the points are.
The coupons I rarely use to be honest. I keep saying I'll start using them more. M: What would make you actually use the coupons?
Sarah: If they were just on. If they applied automatically based on what I'm buying. Like, the till knows I bought Pringles, the app should know there's a Pringles coupon, just give me the money off.
Don't make me do this whole tap-to-activate ritual. I'm tired. M: Anything else about the app that frustrates you?
Sarah: Sometimes it doesn't load. The QR code wheel just spins. And then the cashier is staring at you and there's a queue.

That's stressful. Once I had to actually close the app and reopen it and by then I just used my card and skipped the points which is fine but it's like, the one job you have to do is show a code. M: And the things you genuinely like?
Sarah: The points voucher when it lands. That's a genuinely good moment. £5 off feels like a treat.
The scratch cards are silly but kind of fun, I won't lie. And when I do remember to use a coupon and it works, that's satisfying. Like a tiny win.
M: Last question — if you were redesigning the app, what's the first thing you'd change? Sarah: Make the QR code be on the home screen. Not three taps in.
Just — open app, code is right there. That's it. M: Brilliant.
Thanks Sarah. Sarah: No worries.

T-10847 | verification_stuck | Hi I've been trying to verify my account for THREE DAYS now. I uploaded my passport, took the selfie, did the video. The app keeps saying "verification in progress" but nothing is happening.
I urgently need to access my funds because I have rent due tomorrow. Please help, this is unacceptable. | Hi Maria, thanks for reaching out.
I can see your verification is currently in our review queue. Could you please re-upload a clearer photo of your passport? The current image appears slightly blurred at the edges.
Once you submit a new photo, our team will prioritise the review. | Updated the photo as requested 4 hours ago. Still nothing.

I'm at the point where I'm going to dispute the deposit I made and go back to my old bank. This is the worst onboarding experience I've ever had. | unresolved
T-10912 | transfer_delayed | My GBP transfer to a UK bank account was supposed to arrive instantly via Faster Payments.
It's been 6 hours. The recipient hasn't received it. Reference: 8847221.

Please advise. | Hello, I've checked the transfer reference and can confirm the payment was sent successfully on our side at 14:23 GMT. Sometimes recipient banks have delayed processing, particularly for new payee setups.

I'd recommend asking the recipient to check with their bank. The funds should appear within 24 hours. | resolved
T-10923 | card_abroad | I'm in Thailand right now and my card just got declined at the hotel.
I've travelled here many times before with no issues. Can you check what's happening? I have no cash and the hotel needs payment by midnight local time.
| Hi David, I can see a security flag was triggered due to unusual location activity. I've manually verified the transaction and removed the temporary block. Please try the transaction again.

If it fails, contact us immediately and we'll escalate. | Worked the second time, thank you for the quick response. Just frustrating that I had to contact support at all - I had notified the app I'd be travelling.

| resolved
T-10956 | dispute | I want to dispute a charge for 89.99 GBP from "DIGITAL SUBSCRIPTION CO" on the 14th. I never signed up for this. I think my card details have been compromised.
Please refund and freeze the card. | Hi, I've initiated a dispute on the transaction and frozen your card immediately. A new card will be issued and dispatched within 5-7 business days.

The disputed amount has been provisionally credited back to your account while we investigate. You'll receive an update within 10 business days. | resolved
T-11003 | fees_question | Can someone please explain why I was charged a 2.50 fee for an ATM withdrawal in Spain when I'm on the Plus plan?

Plus plan was supposed to include free ATM withdrawals up to 200 EUR per month. | Hello Linda, the Plus plan includes free ATM withdrawals up to a monthly limit of 200 EUR. Looking at your account, you've withdrawn 247 EUR across 3 transactions this month, exceeding the limit by 47 EUR.
The 2.50 fee is for the portion above the limit. Full details available in our fees schedule in the app. | Ok that makes sense but the app didn't warn me I was approaching the limit.

That would be useful, otherwise I have no way of tracking it. | resolved
T-11078 | premium_cancel | I want to cancel my Metal subscription. I've been paying 14.99 a month for over a year and I barely use any of the perks.

I've already tried to cancel through the app but it keeps showing me retention offers and I just want to stop being charged. | Hi James, I can process the cancellation directly. Your Metal subscription will end on the next billing date (Nov 28).
You'll retain Metal benefits until then. After that, your account will downgrade to Standard. Would you like a confirmation email?

| Yes please send confirmation. And thanks for not making me jump through more hoops to do this. | resolved
T-11102 | suspicious_activity | URGENT - I just got a notification of a 450 GBP transaction to "APPLE.COM/BILL" that I did not make.
My phone is right next to me. Please help block any further charges and reverse this one. I'm panicking.

| Hi, I've immediately blocked your card and frozen your account. The transaction in question is being investigated. Please confirm: do you have any Apple subscriptions or family-shared subscriptions through someone else's Apple ID?
| Oh god you're right. My son has my Apple ID for his iPad and he must have bought something. False alarm.

Please unfreeze. I'm so sorry. | resolved
T-11145 | transfer_delayed | My USD to GBP conversion has been "processing" for 2 days.
Amount: 4500 USD. I need this for a flight tomorrow. The app shows no error, just a spinner.
Why is this taking so long? | Hello, I've located the transaction. It's been held for compliance review due to the size of the conversion.
I've escalated to the team to expedite. You should receive an update within 24 hours. | 24 hours later: still no update.
I've now missed my flight booking deadline. Going to file a formal complaint and consider switching to Wise. This is unacceptable for a financial service.

| escalated
T-11189 | account_closure | I want to close my account permanently. I've been a customer for 4 years but recent issues have made me lose trust. Please process closure and confirm in writing.
I've already withdrawn all my funds. | Hi, sorry to see you go. Before I process the closure, would you mind sharing what specifically led to this decision?
Your feedback helps us improve. If you'd still like to proceed, I'll send a closure form to your registered email within 1 hour. | I appreciate the question.

The main issues were: (1) account frozen for 11 days without clear communication during a compliance review, (2) support response times that don't match the urgency of financial issues, (3) the constant upselling in the app feels disrespectful when basic things don't work. Please proceed with closure. | resolved
T-11234 | verification_stuck | Trying to add my partner as a beneficiary for international transfers.
The verification keeps failing. We've been a couple for 8 years and share an address. Why is this so complicated?

| Hi, I understand the frustration. Adding international beneficiaries requires additional verification under regulatory requirements. Could you confirm: which country is the beneficiary based in, and are you trying to send from a Standard or Premium account?
| Beneficiary is in Romania. I'm on Standard. The thing is the same transfer worked fine 6 months ago to the same person.
Nothing has changed on our end. | unresolved

TKT-001 | how-to | Hi — I just invited 8 people to my project and I'm not sure they know what to do next. Is there anything Asana sends them to help them get started? I don't want to have to walk each person through it manually.

When you invite someone to a project, they receive an email with a link to join. Once they accept, they'll land on the project directly. Asana doesn't send a guided tour to new members by default, but you can share this getting-started guide with your team: [link].

You can also send a message from within Asana by using the Message button in the project header. Let us know if you have other questions! TKT-002 | bug | My recurring task keeps appearing on the wrong day.
I set it to recur weekly but it's showing up on Thursday instead of Monday. I've re-set it twice and it keeps doing this. | Thanks for reaching out!

This is a known behavior — recurring tasks in Asana recur from the date they were completed, not from a fixed day of the week. So if the task was completed on a Thursday, the next instance appears the following Thursday. To get a Monday recurrence, you'd need to complete the task on a Monday (or adjust the due date on the newly created instance before completing it).

We understand this is confusing and have passed this feedback to our product team. Apologies for the friction! TKT-003 | how-to | How do I see all tasks that are overdue across all my projects at once?

I've looked everywhere and can only see overdue tasks one project at a time. | Great question — you can do this with Advanced Search! Click the magnifying glass in the top bar, then click 'Advanced Search.'

Set the filters: Due date = on or before today, Completion = Incomplete, and leave Assignee as 'Any.' This will return all overdue tasks across your workspace. You can also save this search for quick access in the future by clicking 'Save search' after running it.

TKT-004 | feature-request | Can you add a way for project admins to set default notification preferences for team members? Every time I add someone to a project they end up with all notifications on and then they mute everything because it's too much. I want to be able to set something reasonable as the default so new members don't immediately get overwhelmed.

| Thank you for this suggestion! Default notification settings per project is not currently available, but it's a request we hear often. I've logged this as a feature request with your details attached.

In the meantime, you can share our guide on managing notification preferences with new teammates so they can configure them right after joining. We appreciate you taking the time to write in! TKT-005 | how-to | What's the difference between 'Private' and 'Comment-only' access when I invite someone to a project?

The descriptions are confusing. | Happy to clarify! 'Private' refers to the visibility of the project itself — a private project is only visible to members who have been explicitly invited; it won't show up for anyone else in your organization.

'Comment-only' is a permission level for individual members — it means the person can view tasks and leave comments, but cannot create, edit, or delete tasks. You can use both together: a private project where some members have comment-only access. TKT-006 | bug | I duplicated a project to use it as a template for a new project but the copy brought over all the completed tasks.

I only wanted the incomplete tasks and the structure. Is there a setting I missed? | When you duplicate a project in Asana, there is an option to choose what gets copied — tasks, members, and task assignments.

However, filtering by completion status (i.e., only copying incomplete tasks) is not currently an option in the duplication flow. The workaround is to duplicate the project, then manually delete the completed tasks in the copy, or to convert your project into a proper Template (available on Premium and above) which gives you more control over what's included. Sorry for the inconvenience!

TKT-007 | complaint | I've submitted two feature requests in the past year and both times I get the same copy-paste response saying you've 'logged my feedback.' Is there any way to actually track the status of feature requests? I have no idea if anything is being considered.

| I completely understand the frustration, and I want to be transparent with you: we don't currently have a public roadmap where individual feature requests are tracked and visible to users. Your feedback does go to our product team and they review high-volume requests when prioritizing. I know that's not the visibility you're looking for.

If you're interested in participating in our product research panel (where we actually interview customers about upcoming features), I can add you to the list. Would that help? TKT-008 | how-to | Is there a way to assign a task to multiple people?

I have a task that two people are responsible for and I can't figure out how to give it to both of them. | Asana tasks can only have one assignee at a time — this is intentional, as the single-owner model is meant to establish clear accountability. For tasks where multiple people are involved, the recommended approach is to: (1) assign the task to the primary owner, and (2) add the other person as a collaborator (click 'Add followers' in the task detail).

Collaborators get notified of activity on the task but aren't listed as assignees. Alternatively, you can duplicate the task for each person. We know this isn't always ideal and appreciate your patience!

TKT-009 | how-to | Hi, I'm the IT admin at our company. We just rolled out Asana to 200 users. Is there a way to pre-configure Asana settings for all users (like default notification preferences, default project view, etc.)
before they log in for the first time? We're trying to reduce the support burden during rollout. | Welcome to Asana!

For enterprise deployments, some admin-level controls are available through the Admin Console, including SAML SSO, domain claiming, and member management. However, pre-configuring individual user preferences (like notification settings or default views) at the admin level is not currently supported. Each user's preferences are personal and must be set by the user.

Our Enterprise Success team can work with you on a rollout playbook to help guide users through setup — I'd recommend reaching out to your account executive about that resource. TKT-010 | bug | When I'm in a Board view, tasks I complete disappear from the board immediately. This is confusing because I can't see what I just marked done.

It feels like the task got deleted. Can I make completed tasks stay visible on the board? | This is expected behavior — when a task is marked complete in Board view, it moves out of the active column.

It hasn't been deleted; you can find it by enabling 'Show completed tasks' in the View settings (click 'View' at the top right of the project, then toggle 'Completed tasks'). We understand this can be disorienting at first and have passed your feedback to the team about making this behavior clearer during initial setup. TKT-011 | feature-request | Please add the ability to see task count and completion percentage directly on the project card in the sidebar.
When I have 8 projects in my sidebar I have no quick way to assess which ones are behind. I have to click into each one. | Thank you — this is a great idea and you're not the first to request it.

A progress indicator on the sidebar or project list view is something our team has explored. I've added your vote to this request. In the meantime, the Portfolios feature shows completion percentage across projects in a single view — if you're not already using it, it might partially solve this problem.
Happy to send you instructions for setting it up! TKT-012 | complaint | Your Asana Academy is too long. I signed up for a tool, not a certification program.

I just need to know how to do basic things quickly. Can you make a 10-minute version for people who just want to get something done? | Totally fair feedback, and you're not alone — we hear this often.

In addition to Asana Academy, we do have shorter resources: the Quick Start guides (under Help > Get started) are designed for exactly this use case. There are also short video walkthroughs on our YouTube channel for specific tasks like setting up your first project or using My Tasks. I'll also pass your feedback to the team — a 'fast track' onboarding path is something we're actively thinking about.

TKT-013 | how-to | I set up a rule that marks tasks as complete when they're moved to the 'Done' section. But now when I manually complete a task in a different section, it doesn't move to Done. I want it to work both ways — move to Done when complete AND complete when moved to Done.
| You can set up two rules to achieve this! Rule 1: 'When a task is moved to the Done section → Mark the task complete.' Rule 2: 'When a task is marked complete → Move to Done section.'

These rules work together and handle both trigger directions. One thing to watch: if both rules fire at once (e.g., you complete a task that's already in Done), Asana is smart enough not to create a loop, but you may want to test it with a dummy task first. Let me know if you run into any issues!
TKT-014 | bug | I was working in Asana on my phone and marked a task complete. Then I opened the desktop app and the task was still showing as incomplete. I had to refresh the page.

Is this a sync issue? | Thanks for reporting this! In most cases, Asana syncs in real-time between devices, but occasionally there can be a brief delay, especially on mobile if the app goes into the background before the action fully syncs.

A manual refresh (pull-to-refresh on mobile, or F5 on desktop) should always show the current state. If you're seeing consistent delays of more than a minute, it may be worth checking your mobile app version and internet connection. Please let us know if it keeps happening — we'd want to log it as a potential bug.

TKT-015 | how-to | How do I create a project template? I've set up a project structure that works well and I want to reuse it without duplicating the whole project every time. | To create a Template, open your project, click the three-dot menu in the project header, and select 'Save as template.'

You can then access your saved templates when creating a new project — they appear under 'My templates' in the template selection screen. Note: the ability to save custom templates requires a Premium plan or above. If you're on the free plan, project duplication is the best alternative for now.

Happy to walk you through either option! TKT-016 | complaint | I find it really hard to explain to new team members what the difference between a Task, Subtask, Sub-subtask, and Section is. The hierarchy makes sense to me now but it confused everyone I've added to the team.

Is there a visual explainer I can share with them? | This is a common sticking point and you're right that the hierarchy can be confusing at first! We have a visual overview of Asana's structure here: [help link].

The short version: Sections are headers that organize tasks within a project; Tasks are the core unit of work; Subtasks break a task into smaller pieces; Sub-subtasks go one level deeper (though we generally recommend avoiding this level of nesting as tasks can become hard to find). I'll also pass your feedback — a clearer in-product explanation for new members is something we're aware of. TKT-017 | feature-request | I manage multiple clients as a freelancer and I use separate Asana workspaces for each one.
The biggest problem is that I can't see my tasks across all workspaces in one place. I have to switch workspaces to check each one. This is a huge daily pain point.

Is cross-workspace task view something you're planning? | Thank you for sharing this — it's a real pain point for freelancers and consultants who manage multiple workspaces. Currently, My Tasks and cross-project views are scoped to a single workspace.

I don't have timeline information I can share on whether cross-workspace views are planned, but this is one of our most-requested features from independent practitioners and I've added your name to the request. In the meantime, some users consolidate client work into a single workspace using separate teams and privacy settings, though I know that's not ideal for everyone. TKT-018 | how-to | I accidentally deleted a task.
Can I undo this? I don't see an undo button anywhere. | Yes, you can restore it!

Deleted tasks go to your project's Trash, accessible from the three-dot menu in the project header — click 'View trash.' From there you can restore any recently deleted task. You can also use Ctrl+Z (or Cmd+Z on Mac) immediately after deletion to undo it, as long as you haven't navigated away.

If the task has been in trash for more than 30 days it will be permanently deleted, but you likely have plenty of time. Let me know if you can't find it! TKT-019 | complaint | We're a team of 5 and we hit the 15-project limit on the free plan.
We can't afford the Business tier ($$$) but the free plan is too restricted. Is there a small team or startup pricing option? The pricing gap between free and paid feels enormous.

| Thank you for writing in, and I hear you on the pricing gap. We do offer a discounted rate for eligible nonprofits, and there's an annual billing option that reduces the monthly cost. For very small teams, the Premium plan (lower than Business) may be worth reviewing — it includes unlimited projects without some of the more advanced features.

I'd encourage you to speak with our sales team as they sometimes have options for early-stage startups. I'm sorry I can't offer a specific discount here, but I want to make sure you have all the options. TKT-020 | how-to | What does the little lightning bolt icon mean on some tasks?

I've seen it appear and disappear and I have no idea what triggers it. | The lightning bolt icon indicates that the task was created or modified by an automation rule (also called a 'trigger action'). It's a signal that something automated happened to that task rather than a manual user action.

If you're seeing it unexpectedly, it means a rule in your project fired on that task — you can review your project's rules under 'Customize' to see which rules are active. If you didn't set up any rules intentionally, it's possible a template or someone else with edit access created one. Let me know if you'd like help finding it!

TKT-021-thread | data-export | Hi — we're planning to cancel our subscription and I need to export all our project data first. We have about 12 active projects and 3 years of completed work. Can you walk me through how to export everything?

I want task names, descriptions, comments, assignees, and due dates if possible. || FOLLOW-UP (2 days later): Thanks for the instructions — I followed the steps but the export only seems to include tasks from one project at a time. I have 12 projects.
Is there a way to export everything in one go? Also the CSV doesn't include task comments, just the other fields. Is that normal?
| REPLY 1: Hi! To export a project, open it, click the three-dot menu in the project header, and select 'Export / Print.' You can choose CSV or JSON.
The CSV includes task names, assignees, due dates, sections, and descriptions. For comments, the JSON format is more complete. Repeat this for each project.

If you're on Business or above, you may also have access to the Admin Console for broader data exports. Let me know how it goes! || REPLY 2: You're correct — project exports are per-project, and there's no bulk export for all projects simultaneously.

For 12 projects you would need to export each one individually. Regarding comments: CSV exports do not include comments; the JSON export includes the full task object with comments attached. If you need a single combined file, you'd need to merge the JSON exports manually or use the Asana API (documentation here: [link]).

I understand this isn't the smoothest process for a large export — I've flagged this as feedback to our product team. I'm sorry the offboarding experience wasn't smoother. TKT-022-thread | billing | I'm trying to understand our invoice.

We have 14 seats but the invoice shows 17. We didn't add anyone this month — where are the extra 3 seats coming from? || FOLLOW-UP (1 day later): Thanks for the explanation about guest vs member seats.

Quick follow-up: two of the 'members' you identified are contractors who only need occasional access. Can I downgrade them to guests without losing their task history or comments? I want to make sure we don't accidentally lose any of their work before I change anything.

| REPLY 1: Happy to help clarify! In Asana, a 'seat' refers to a full member of your workspace — anyone who has been added as a member (not a guest). Guests have limited access and don't count toward your seat total.

Looking at your account, it appears 3 users were upgraded from guest to member status in the past billing cycle — this can happen if a guest accepts certain types of project invitations that trigger a membership upgrade. I'll list those 3 accounts in a follow-up message so you can confirm. || REPLY 2: Good news — you can convert a full member back to a guest without losing their task history, comments, or assignments.

Their completed and open tasks remain intact; they just lose the ability to create projects or access workspace-level features. To do this: go to Admin Console > Members, find the user, and change their role to Guest. I'd still recommend reviewing their open task assignments before downgrading, just to make sure nothing falls through the cracks.
Let me know if you need help walking through it! TKT-023-thread | onboarding | We have a new employee starting Monday. I want to make sure she's set up in Asana and knows what to do on her first day.

What's the best way to onboard a new team member? Should I add her before she starts, assign her tasks in advance, and is there any kind of welcome flow she'll go through when she first logs in? || FOLLOW-UP (4 days later): Quick update — she joined on Monday and said the experience was confusing.

She accepted the invite, got into Asana, saw a project, and had no idea what she was supposed to do first. Is there something I should have done differently to set her up better? She also said she got a lot of emails immediately and nearly unsubscribed from all notifications.

| REPLY 1: Great that you're thinking about this in advance! Here's what I'd recommend: (1) Add her to Asana before Monday so she can accept the invite and get oriented. (2) Assign her a few concrete tasks with due dates in the first week — having her name on specific tasks makes My Tasks useful immediately.

(3) Consider writing a brief comment on her first task explaining what you want her to do and how you're using Asana as a team. (4) Send her this quick-start guide [link] along with the invite. There's no formal welcome flow for new members beyond the invite email, so the context you set up manually matters a lot.

|| REPLY 2: Thanks for the follow-up — what you're describing is unfortunately a common experience. The new member invite flow doesn't include an orientation or a 'here's what to do first' prompt. A few things that help: (1) When you add someone to a project, leave a welcome comment on their first task.

(2) Ask her to go to My Tasks first (not Home or a project) — it's the cleanest view for someone just starting out. (3) For notifications: walk her through Settings > Notifications and turn off email notifications for everything except @mentions and task assignments. That usually cuts the noise dramatically.
I'd also pass along that a guided new-member experience is something we hear often as a gap — your feedback has been logged.

Asana completely changed how our marketing team operates. Before, we were drowning in Slack threads and missed deadlines. Now everything has a home.

The timeline view is beautiful for campaign planning and my team actually checks it. Took about 3 weeks to fully adopt but so worth it. | 2025-04-03 | Marketing Manager
Onboarding is a nightmare if you have a distributed team.

| 2025-05-17 | Operations Lead
Really powerful once you get past the learning curve. Custom fields and automations are genuinely game-changing for our ops workflows. But I'll be honest — the learning curve is steep and I nearly quit in the first month.

The payoff is real though. | 2025-06-02 | Project Manager
We churned after 6 weeks. The mobile experience is so bad that our field team just refused to use it.

Half our team is on-site, not at a desk. If mobile was on par with desktop we'd probably still be customers. | 2025-06-29 | Operations Manager
The best project management tool I've used in 15 years of product management.

Portfolios and workload views are exactly what I need to manage multiple teams. The AI task suggestions are hit or miss but I appreciate that they're trying. | 2025-07-11 | VP of Product
Mixed feelings.
The tool itself is excellent. The pricing is not. We're a 12-person team and the jump from the free plan to Business tier felt extreme.

There are features I need — like custom rules — that are locked behind a tier that's priced for companies 10x our size. | 2025-07-24 | Startup Founder
Solid tool. My only real complaint is that the onboarding flow guides the admin (me) but does nothing for the teammates I invited.

They just got dropped into a project with no context. I had to personally walk each of them through it. | 2025-08-05 | Engineering Lead
I've used Asana at three different companies and every time the same thing happens: it gets adopted by one team, partially used by another team, and ignored by a third.

The cross-team adoption problem is never solved. It's always a political project, not just a software project. | 2025-08-19 | Program Manager
My freelance business runs on Asana.
Client projects, my own admin, content calendar, invoicing reminders — all of it. The My Tasks view keeps me sane. I don't know how I managed clients before this.

| 2025-09-01 | Freelance Consultant
Good product but the template library is overwhelming. I spent 45 minutes trying to pick a template and ended up using a blank project anyway. You need better filtering or a wizard that recommends templates based on what you're actually trying to do.

| 2025-09-14 | Content Strategist
Notifications are completely broken for our use case. We turned on Slack integration and our #ops-updates channel got flooded within a day. Turned it off.

Then no one knew what was happening. There's no middle ground. | 2025-09-28 | Head of Operations
After 6 months of use I can say this is genuinely the best tool for keeping cross-functional projects on track.

Marketing, engineering, and CS all use the same project board and for the first time everyone knows what everyone else is working on. Transformative. | 2025-10-10 | Chief of Staff
Really good for structured work.
Less good for exploratory or creative work where the scope isn't clear yet. We use it alongside Notion — Asana for execution, Notion for thinking. That combo works well.

| 2025-10-22 | Product Designer
The recurring task feature is fundamentally confusing. I've read the help article three times and I still get surprised by when the next instance appears. It seems to recur from completion date not from a fixed schedule, which is almost never what I want.

| 2025-11-04 | Operations Coordinator
Asana saved our Q4 launch. 15 people, 6 workstreams, 8-week timeline. Everything was tracked, everyone knew their role, nothing fell through the cracks.

I've managed launches without a tool like this and I will never go back. | 2025-11-18 | Product Marketing Manager
The reporting features are good if you find them, which takes a while. I didn't know about the advanced search until month 2.

Once I started using saved searches and custom reports my experience improved significantly. But why do I have to discover this on my own? | 2025-12-01 | Project Manager
Canceled after 30 days.

We're a 6-person engineering team and Asana is just not built for how engineers work. No story points, no sprint concept, no GitHub integration that actually works in our workflow. We went back to Linear.

| 2025-12-15 | Engineering Manager

Incredible tool for scaling a growing team.
When we were 10 people we used Slack and spreadsheets. At 40 people everything was on fire. Asana brought order.

The onboarding took a month to fully settle but I'd do it again without question. | 2026-02-03 | COO

What works: timeline, custom fields, automations, portfolio view.

It needs a 'show all nested tasks' setting. | 2026-03-02 | Senior Project Manager

Five stars for the mobile app update.
I manage field teams and the previous mobile experience was unusable. The new version is much better. My team actually uses it on-site now which I thought was impossible six months ago.

| 2026-03-18 | Field Operations Manager
Pros:
- Timeline view is genuinely excellent once your tasks have dates on them
- Custom fields give real flexibility without needing a developer
- My Tasks is the best personal task view I've used in any PM tool

Cons:
- Mobile app noticeably weaker than desktop, especially for non-desk workers
- New member onboarding is essentially nonexistent — people get dropped in cold
- Price jump from Free to Business is steep; features I actually need are all on Business

Bottom line: worth it if you have a dedicated person to maintain it. Too much if everyone is expected to self-manage. | 2025-08-28 | Operations Manager
What I wish I'd known before signing up:
- The onboarding checklist is easy to skip and most people skip it — don't let your team do this
- 'My Tasks' is the key to individual adoption; lead with this when introducing it to your team
- Automations/rules are the highest-leverage feature but nobody finds them in the first month
- Pricing: per-seat costs add up fast if you have contractors or seasonal staff who need access

Still recommending it.

Just go in with realistic expectations about the ramp-up time. | 2025-10-30 | Program Manager
We're a 2-person design studio. We tried Asana for 3 weeks.
Complete overkill for our needs. Half the features we'd never touch, and the ones we wanted were locked behind a paid tier. We went back to a shared Notion page.

Asana is clearly built for teams of 20+, not for a duo trying to manage client projects without losing their minds. | 2025-07-06 | Freelance Designer
Pros:
- The product itself is genuinely good when it works
- Timeline and portfolio views are best-in-class

Cons:
- Seat pricing is punishing for companies with seasonal or temporary workers
- We hire 15 extra people every Q4 for 8 weeks — there's no good answer for this in Asana
- Guest access is too limited to be a real workaround; full member seats are too expensive for temp workers
- Ended up with team leads doing double-entry to avoid the seat cost, which defeated the entire point

Would upgrade my rating if they added a 'temporary contributor' seat type at a lower price point. | 2026-01-15 | Operations Manager
Six months in.

Honest assessment:

What works well:
- Cross-functional visibility — marketing, product, and CS finally see each other's work
- Automations once you figure them out (took me 6 weeks)
- Reporting for executive updates

What still frustrates me:
- New members get no onboarding — I have to do it manually every time someone joins
- Notification defaults are too aggressive; new users always complain before they figure out settings
- Help center vocabulary doesn't match how non-PM people describe their problems

Net: I'd buy it again but I'd set aside a full month for proper rollout instead of expecting people to self-serve. | 2026-02-25 | Head of Product
